title: Nine Judges, Two Effective Votes: Correlated Errors Undermine LLM Evaluation Panels
gist: A 9-LLM judge panel from 7 model families delivers only ~2 independent votes' worth of information because models make correlated errors on the same items, causing actual accuracy to fall 8-22 percentage points short of the independent-voting ideal. Neither adding judges nor smarter aggregation closes the gap, undermining the core premise of LLM-as-a-judge ensemble evaluation.

themes.theme: Effective independence collapse in LLM judge panels

insights.claim: A panel of 9 frontier LLMs drawn from 7 model families effectively provides only about 2 independent votes' worth of information when judging natural language inference tasks.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Buyers of AI evaluation tooling should not assume ensemble diversity equals reliability; vendor claims of multi-model judging panels may be marketing more independence than exists.
insights.quote: the 9 judges effectively provide only about 2 independent votes’ worth of information

insights.claim: Roughly three-quarters of an LLM judge panel's nominal independence is lost because the models make the same mistakes on the same items.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Correlated model errors are the binding constraint on eval quality, meaning genuine architectural or training diversity matters far more than panel size.
insights.quote: Roughly three-quarters of the panel’s nominal independence is lost because the models make the same mistakes on the same items.

insights.claim: An LLM judge panel's actual accuracy falls 8 to 22 percentage points short of what independent voting would achieve.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: The reliability gap is large enough to materially mislead model rankings and benchmark leaderboards built on judge panels.
insights.quote: the panel’s actual accuracy falls 8–22 percentage points short of what independent voting would achieve

insights.claim: The single best judge model matches or outperforms the full multi-model panel across all tested conditions.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Spending compute on running many judges is wasteful; identifying and using one strong judge can be cheaper and at least as accurate.
insights.quote: the best single judge matches or outperforms the full panel across all conditions

themes.theme: Aggregation algorithms cannot rescue correlated panels

insights.claim: Established aggregation methods close at most 11% of the panel accuracy gap, even when given access to the correct answers.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Investing in smarter vote-aggregation software is a dead end; the problem is upstream in the correlated judges themselves.
insights.quote: established methods close at most 11% of this gap, even with access to the correct answers

insights.claim: The reliability deficit persists across prompt variants, temperatures, chain-of-thought reasoning, and a pairwise preference task on RewardBench, indicating it is not an artifact of a single setup.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: The finding is robust enough to generalize across common evaluation configurations, so practitioners cannot prompt-engineer their way around it.

insights.claim: The study quantifies panel reliability using the Kish effective sample size (n_eff) and a Condorcet null model to compare observed against ideal independent voting.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Provides a reusable metric for auditing whether any ensemble evaluation system delivers its claimed statistical independence.

insights.claim: Scaling up the number of judges cannot substitute for genuinely independent evaluation because the bottleneck is correlated judges rather than the aggregation algorithm.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI evaluation roadmap toward sourcing diverse, decorrelated judges rather than scaling panel headcount.
insights.quote: scaling up panels cannot substitute for genuinely independent evaluation
